then do you, we'll talk about you for most of this time, and then I have, like, a list of questions that I can ask.

sorry.

We just did like a reorg of our teams.
But at our heart right now, we are a marketing agency sort of services model, but we're built on top of an internal platform that generates all of our content using AI workflows.
For clarity, we do not do like social YouTube paid ads, anything like that.
We're just organic blog content.
And so that like agency model should be like a
It is a pretty traditional pod structure where we have an engagement manager managing eight accounts today will scale to 12.
And then we have a production layer, which is content managers or managing editors who own end-to-end production of content for two to three accounts.
So they're working in the pipelines, generating all that content, and then sending it out to clients or getting it ready to publish.
The engagement managers are working on strategy and obviously relationships and renewals are your main KPIs.
So relationship strength leading to renewals, hopefully expansions down the line.
And then we have a layer of directors slash specialists who can step in if you need.

More help with things like technical SEO read or things like that, technical SEO evaluation, or like a full shift in content strategy, and you just like need a partner to help with like a long-term roadmapping.
We do have like an onboarding pod, or onboarding, yeah, pod, like a strategy sprint.
We call it a strategy sprint.
So for the first eight weeks, every client goes through a strategy sprint.
We have engagement managers there as well.
That is much, a much more like strategic side of the business because you're sort of going zero to one in terms of how the, what the client engagement will look like.
Like, I'm not sure if you talked to Marilia or anybody about kind of like where in that you, like you've sat before or what that experience looks like.
Would love to dive in a little bit there because like...

It's the same role engagement managers like through onboarding and then engagement managers kind of post onboarding with long term account management type, but obviously, the focus and like, KPIs are a little bit different.
Yeah, so that's like what the delivery org looks like.
I think long term, we are going to move like to be to have a product offering.
So our platform will be external facing as well.
So there's like a lot of opportunity on that side as well.

you.

I've helped build SEO teams from the ground up.

And kind of like hire, onboard, and train those SEOs as well.
On the in-house side, I was the first ever SEO manager hired at Teachable.
If you're familiar with that platform.
And so I built the whole SEO structure there, KPIs, everything.
And in the last couple of years, I've kind of been consulting.
So I work with mainly a lot of travel brands.
Like I have Norwegian Cruise Line, American Airlines.
I do a lot of different consulting jobs for them.
Like mostly focused on content and technical SEO as well.
So my background is very much SEO manager related in more recent years.
last five?
going analyze a you.

I've run everything from onboarding calls, like you were mentioning, to ongoing like month-to-month, you know, account management kind of things.
I also build the strategies myself.
So I'm very good at presenting those strategies to the clients on an ongoing basis, going over performance.
Let me see.
Am I missing anything?
Yeah, I'm very savvy with handling different types of clients as well.
I have clients that have very large teams.
I jump on a call and there's like 20 people from their team and I need to explain like complex SEO to them in a non-technical way.
I've also had clients that are very new.
There's like a couple of people on their team.
They're very new to SEO and trying to discover what they should do.
So yeah, I've done small.
You know, SEO projects, two very large ones as well.
So, yeah.
Amazing.
Cool.

Well, can you talk to me about, like, managing, I mean, I know you've been sort of a consultant on your end, so maybe not within your own company, but, like, projects that have required you to work, like, with multiple stakeholders across an org.
It sounds like you've got some of that, but I think, like, getting buy-in from, you know, different roles and different parts of an organization for work that you're doing.
Yeah, absolutely.
So, I think the best example would be the Teachable job, because SEO was very new to the company.
People kind of didn't know what to expect.
They knew SEO was important, but what are we going to get from it?
What are the results we're going to get?
What kind of budgets do we need?
Et cetera, et cetera.
So, that's the best example.
I also had to.
Collaborate a lot with engineering, content, the dev team, the brand team.
We had contractors.
We had an agency that used to do some of the part of the SEO work as well before.
So I kind of like came in like a parachute into that situation.
So I think the first one was sending, you know, looking at what kind of strategy we needed for the company.
So doing a very deep dive audit, setting a six-month plan.
And then presenting that to our internal team, you know, including executive leadership.
Delivering what kind of results we can get from SEO within this first year of work.
And then asking for the budgets that I needed to get that done.
And then after that, after I got the sign off on that, it was building workflows with those different teams that I had to work with consistently.
So I built the system on monday.com, kind of like a project management flow that used to just...
Pass through different teams as we started consistently working together and just kind of made that better over time to make it smooth for everybody.
Amazing.
Just taking notes here.
What do you think was like the real key differentiator between success and failure in that like work?
Oh, that's a great question.
So I think it was making sure we set goals that actually impacted business outcomes through the SEO work that we were doing.
So it's very easy to get kind of sucked into the vanity metrics and say, oh, we are driving X amount of more traffic or X amount of more keywords are ranking better.
You know, those are great.
And those are kind of like the starting point.
But the real difference was how is SEO tying into more leads, more...
What's the ROI from the SEO efforts that we're doing?
I think that's what made it more impactful within that first year.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Keeps people bought in.
Okay.
And then how do you think about like organization across multiple accounts and also like across multiple teams, keeping everybody on track?
Yeah.
So I usually use project management tools and I work from the deadline and kind of work backwards.
So I start with dividing it by month and then every Friday I kind of make my plan for the next week.
So I kind of use that tool to kind of map out Monday through Friday, what that next week is going to look like based on priorities.
So the way I look at priorities is I have this kind of like little matrix that I do.
What is, you know, high, you know, low lift, high impact.
That's where I start.
And then what is high-lift, low-impact, and those I keep for, you know, another part of the project.
So I kind of like prioritize all of the asks and insert that into my project management workflow.
Yeah.
Cool.
Okay.
What PM tools have you used in the past?
Monday.com, ClickUp, Jira, Trello, you name it.
Do you have anything that you prefer?
I know we're sort of evaluating that right now, too.
So I'm curious, like, where have you found success?
What, like, what's the, like, must-have for a tool like that that you're definitely always using?
To be honest, I think they're all the same.
It's how you create the workflow within that tool that will make your life easier or not.
Yeah.
I that's the.
I think they all have very similar functions.
It's just how you build your own workflow that will make the difference.
So I think I've had disastrous like Monday.com setups, and I've had really good ones, you know, so it's not the tool, it's the workflow.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, totally.
Okay, and then can you talk to me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track and what you did to get it back?
Yeah, sure.
Let me think of a good example.
So you mean off track from internally, like we were supposed to deliver something and we were late, or something went off track from our side?
Either, I think.

Like, yeah, like when something's happening that you know is going to impact like the sort of account health score overall, like it could be an internal thing, it could be like they're pivoting and you guys have to scramble.
So, like anything that could put the account at risk as a result.
Yeah, that makes sense.
So I'll give a recent example.
So one of the clients that I work with has quite a large internal team themselves that I regularly work with.
We usually have like over 10 people on our regular like biweekly calls.
So I do work very closely with the dev team on their side, which is an internal team.
And from an SEO perspective, sometimes when we recommend things that are very heavy lift, this one person does a lot of pushback on making those happen.
And sometimes the reasons that are provided are not really the real reasons.
It's just pushback because they're very overloaded and they don't necessarily want to do it.
So I've had a lot of those situations with the same person on the team.
And so the way I look at it is like I try and be kind of like the calming force in the situation.
Not take it personally and just try to absorb it.
And instead of saying, okay, your reasons are ridiculous.
No, like I understand your reasons.
Let's think of maybe two more solutions that can be maybe easier for your team to implement.
However, I also like to present what that would mean for results.
Like our recommendation is option number one.
If you don't go with this recommendation and go with option two, for example, the results are not going to be as significant as our first recommendation.
So just kind of setting expectations on what's going to happen if you do change our primary strategy.
Yeah.
And like what extent did that, what kind of effect did that have?
Like how did it work?
When did it not work?
So sometimes it results in many more meetings where there needs to be many more discussions about all of these solutions that we offer.
Um.
Which is fine.
Sometimes you need to do it.
But other times when I've offered other solutions like that, it's essentially up to the client at the end of the day.
They might come back and say, okay, even if this is not getting the results that we were expecting initially, we're fine with this.
We're comfortable with the second option.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah, I think those are all my questions.
I'm curious what questions you have for me.
Yeah.
So I have a few questions written down.
So, okay.
So what would you say?
A is the biggest challenge for an engagement manager today.
Yeah, I think while the role is like really being defined, didn't exist until three weeks ago.
So it's like account management.
I think like balancing, you're not in editorial at all, like you're not, but you still have to kind of represent the output.

And so I think right now we're trying to figure out how we balance like engagement managers, like enabling the feedback loop for editorial and making sure that editorial output is high quality without like getting sucked into the day to day of like that editorial output.
So I think that's a lot of like context sharing, making sure that we are putting processes in place for like managing editors in the production layer.
And then, yeah.

Like we, my priority for the next few weeks is like processes, templates, making sure that they engagement managers have all the tools that they need to like actually have like a repeatable sort of predictable process in place.
So it's a little bit of the Wild West right now, I would say.
So like working in uncertainty, quite honestly, is something that's like super important right now.
I get that.
I have been there for sure.
Okay.
And how would you measure success for this role?
You mentioned it briefly, but I'm just wondering, like, do you have KPIs in place already?
So you're like literally renewals is like P0.
So just making sure that like we are actually retaining the clients that we're,
We have today.
And then we do have like account health metrics that feed into that, that are qualitative.
It's like a qualitative fill in the numbers.

So like there isn't something automatically that like creates these numbers, but it's relationship, performance against goals, quality of like content, which obviously some of these you don't have like direct oversight into, but that's kind of why I was saying it's like a challenge of finding that balance.
And then like quantity in terms of like, are we delivering against contract, like the number of articles that we said that we would and things like that.
Right.

Growthx.
The client is looking to growthx to set that strategy.
Got it.
Makes sense.

Um, and yeah, it's very, very nonstop.
Um, so you have to, like, be ready for uncertainty and, like, know that, like, it's, there's something around every corner.
Um, but it's nonstop because we're, like, moving really fast and we have great signals.
Like, a lot of, like, really positive, uh, like, excitement from clients.
Like, what's happening on our product is super exciting.
So, like, it's nonstop because there's really cool stuff happening.
Um, and it's an amazing thing to be a part of right now.
Um, but you have to.
To be ready to, like, really dive in and immerse yourself.
Yeah, that makes sense.

Yeah, awesome.
